- "He knows that look—rage sharpened to a point. Revenge."
- “You don’t know me,” the man says, flicking his eyes toward the mud-stained portrait. “But you might remember my wife.” … “You burned her at the stake.”

story:
Inquisitor Gregor Ezekyle Kern, a hard man who mistakes cruelty for righteousness, watches his two hulking escorts drag an accused witch from her reeking hut. He thumbs through his Latin scripture and stops on the cleansing verse all inquisitors use before a killing. The woman collapses to her knees, sobbing for mercy. Kern smiles. He likes when the condemned beg. He raises his voice to pass sentence—death—when a man in a gray highwayman’s coat clears his throat behind him.

Hat brim low, messenger bag at his side, pistols strapped across his chest, the stranger holds out an envelope. “Inquisitor Kern? Message from the Grand Master.” Kern snatches it, dismissing him, and finds only a hand-drawn portrait of a beautiful woman. No orders. No instructions. He frowns, puzzled. Behind him, one guard yelps that the witch bit him. The other cocks back a fist, then picks up a rock to teach her a lesson.

Two pistol shots crack the air. Both escorts drop. The witch bolts for the treeline. Kern pivots. The “messenger” stands with smoking pistols, tosses them aside, and draws a rapier. “Draw your sword, old man.” Kern sets his book on the grass, slides out his blade, and meets the stranger’s gaze. He knows not only the rage but its source: the woman in the portrait is this man’s wife, and he has come to avenge Kern’s burning of her.

Steel meets steel. The messenger beats the blade; Kern parries and ripostes. He lunges; the strike is turned aside. “You have good form. Who are you?” The man doesn’t answer. He presses forward, forcing Kern to stumble over a corpse, but the inquisitor recovers and scores a cut on the stranger’s shoulder. Kern twirls his rapier, amused. “A noble, perhaps?” The messenger stares at the blood on his palm as if he cannot believe he’s been touched. Kern attacks, his blade ringing off the other’s with each stroke. They break apart, both breathing hard. Kern, almost pleased to have found a worthy foe, asks again who he is.

“You don’t know me,” the man says, flicking his eyes toward the mud-stained portrait. “But you might remember my wife.” Kern hesitates; the face is familiar. “You burned her at the stake.” Kern answers coldly that he has executed many heretics. The messenger’s next cut buries the tip of his rapier in the wet earth. He yanks; it’s stuck. Kern smiles and slashes down the man’s back, driving him to his knees.

Blade beneath the man’s chin, Kern lifts his face so their eyes meet. “Your revenge ends here. If I killed her, she deserved it.” The messenger grabs the edge of the blade with his bare hand in a last, desperate act. Kern jerks free; fingers fall, blood sheets, strength drains. The man collapses into the mud and goes still.

Kern surveys the bodies—the fallen stranger, his own wasted hirelings—and sighs at the mess. He spots the witch’s tracks, a clear line into the forest. He sheathes his rapier, retrieves his book, and, with petty malice, grinds the portrait of the dead wife into the mud beneath his boot. Then he follows the tracks, intent on finishing what he began.
